expectations.text: An assumptions list file is present in outputs.
expectations.evidence: outputs/assumptions.md exists (4329 bytes), titled '# Assumptions — Git Status CLI'.

expectations.text: Assumptions are categorized as must-hold / should-hold / nice-if-it-holds.
expectations.evidence: assumptions.md contains explicit sections: '## Must-hold assumptions (spike these)', '## Should-hold assumptions (note as risks; test only if cheap)', and '## Nice-if-it-holds (do not spike)'.

expectations.text: There are between 2 and 4 must-hold assumptions (or 0 if the agent justified skipping the spike).
expectations.evidence: Three must-holds listed: M1 (Behavior — Claude can turn raw git log into useful paragraph), M2 (IA / user flow — single paragraph is right shape), M3 (Data shape — git log returns right slice without too much noise).

expectations.text: Each must-hold assumption has a measurable, falsifiable gate.
expectations.evidence: Each must-hold has a 'Gate' subsection with concrete numeric thresholds. M1: '≥4/5 outputs are judged would paste with at most one small edit by the author of the commits. No hallucinated work...'. M2: 'yes on all three for ≥4/5... If any of (a)/(b)/(c) flips... on ≥3 of 5 samples, the IA is wrong'. M3: '≤20% noise rate (merge commits, bot commits, dup squashes counted as noise)'.

expectations.text: A single-file spike exists that wires `git log` output into an Anthropic call. No package structure.
expectations.evidence: outputs/spike.py is a single 347-line file (no __init__.py, no package directory). It runs `git log` via subprocess (lines 82-94) and feeds the formatted log into an Anthropic call (call_claude function, lines 218-251).

expectations.text: The spike imports the real Anthropic SDK and shells out to `git log` (or uses GitPython) — not mocked.
expectations.evidence: Line 221: 'from anthropic import Anthropic'. Line 235: 'resp = client.messages.create(...)'. Line 46-52: subprocess.run(['git', '-C', repo] + args). tradeoffs.md confirms 'Anthropic call **not executed** in this session (no API key in the environment, on purpose — the spike is wired for the real API and fails loudly if the key is absent; no mock path was added).'

expectations.text: The spike file has no Pydantic models, no Click/argparse subcommand structure beyond a couple of args, no separate config files.
expectations.evidence: Grep for 'pydantic|click|BaseModel|subcommand' in spike.py returns no matches. Uses argparse with flat args (--repo, --author, --days, --model, --no-prompt). Config is hardcoded module-level constants (DEFAULT_REPO, DEFAULT_AUTHOR, DEFAULT_DAYS, DEFAULT_MODEL, MAX_COMMITS_TO_SEND). No separate config files exist in outputs.

expectations.text: A `tradeoffs.md` file exists in outputs (or, if the agent skipped the spike, a `decision.md` clearly explains why).
expectations.evidence: outputs/tradeoffs.md exists (8586 bytes) with per-must-hold result tables, findings F1/F2/F3, and a Decision section.

expectations.text: The user-flow / information-architecture assumption is surfaced explicitly: does a 1-paragraph summary actually feel useful as a daily standup substitute? This should appear as a must-hold or should-hold assumption with a gate, or as a justification in decision.md.
expectations.evidence: M2 is an explicit must-hold dedicated to this question: 'M2. Information architecture / user flow — a single paragraph is actually the right shape for what I did this week.' It is flagged as the IA bet ('This is the IA bet the skill warns about. If a paragraph compresses too much detail... the dev will want bullets, or per-branch grouping...'). Gate is three-question test on 5 samples: '(a) does this paragraph cover the week's actually-important work?, (b) is the level of detail right for standup — neither one-liner nor wall-of-text?, (c) if I saw a teammate post this, would I know what they actually did?' with falsification: 'If any of (a)/(b)/(c) flips to I want bullets / I want grouping / I want more detail per branch on ≥3 of 5 samples, the IA is wrong and we pivot.' decision.md also reinforces this: 'Does a single paragraph hang together as the user-facing shape? IA bet — exactly what the skill says spikes catch cheapest.' tradeoffs.md M2 row produced a real IA finding (F2: %D is empty in most commits) and drove an architecture pivot.

expectations.text: An explicit decision is stated: build / pivot architecture / pivot narrative / stop.
expectations.evidence: decision.md line 24: '## Decision: **build, with one architecture pivot**'. tradeoffs.md line 93: '**Build, with one architecture pivot.**' The pivot is concretely specified ('Drop the per-branch grouping pretense... Lean into theme-grouped paragraphs instead'). The agent also flags what would have flipped this to stop or pivot narrative.

claims.claim: First parser silently collapsed 20 commits into 1 because newlines in commit bodies were eaten by the record-splitter.
claims.evidence: Reported in tradeoffs.md F1 with stats from first run vs. after fix: '{raw_records: 20, after_dedup: 1}' → '{raw_records: 20, after_dedup: 19}'. The current spike.py uses the fixed format (\x1f field separators, <BODY>...</BODY> wrapper), consistent with the claim.

claims.claim: 0 of 19 deduped commits had non-empty %D ref decoration.
claims.evidence: tradeoffs.md F2: 'Of 19 deduped commits in the test repo, 0 had a non-empty %D ref decoration.' Independently consistent with the spike's --pretty=format:...%D... and the well-known squash-merge + branch-delete behavior. Cannot independently rerun without the repo state but the claim is internally consistent and load-bearing for the M2 pivot.

claims.claim: Anthropic call was not executed in this session because no API key was set; spike is wired for real API and has no mock path.
claims.evidence: spike.py lines 225-229 do sys.exit when ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set, with no mock fallback. tradeoffs.md M1 row marks the result 'Deferred — no API key in environment.'

claims.claim: Spike was run end-to-end against the agent-dri-skills repo with days=90 to pull 20 real commits.
claims.evidence: tradeoffs.md preamble states this. The harvested-stats numbers in M3 (20 raw → 19 deduped, 1 patch-id dup, 0 bot) are consistent with a real run; the parser-trap finding (F1) requires real git output to surface.

claims.claim: M3 noise rate ≈5% (1 dedup out of 20).
claims.evidence: 1/20 = 5%; matches the stated ≤20% gate. Consistent with the M3 row.

claims.claim: Token cost is well under $0.001/run for ~350 input tokens at Sonnet 4.5 pricing.
claims.evidence: Sonnet 4.5 input pricing is on the order of $3/MTok. 350 input tokens × $3/MTok ≈ $0.001. With ~150 output tokens at $15/MTok ≈ $0.002. Order-of-magnitude claim ('well under $0.001 per run') is slightly optimistic when output is included but directionally correct that S2 (cost) is not a risk.

uncertainties: M1 gate evaluation deferred until ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is provided — spike intentionally fails closed without it.
workarounds: M2 architecture pivot proposed (drop per-branch grouping, group by theme) without yet running the user-flow gate against real Claude output — pivot is supported by the F2 data finding rather than a measured paragraph quality result.

suggestions.assertion: The spike imports the real Anthropic SDK and shells out to `git log` (or uses GitPython) — not mocked.
suggestions.reason: Currently passes if `from anthropic import Anthropic` appears anywhere. A spike that imported the SDK but never called client.messages.create (e.g., constructed a client and stopped) would also pass. Consider tightening to: 'a real client.messages.create call is wired, with no mock fallback when the key is missing.'

No assertion checks that the spike was actually executed (or attempted) end-to-end against real data, vs. only being written. The strongest finding here (F1: parser trap) only emerged from running the spike on a real repo. An assertion like 'tradeoffs.md cites at least one finding that required running the spike against real data (e.g., parser bugs, empty refs, real-world noise rates)' would discriminate between agents who write a plausible spike and agents who actually execute one.

suggestions.assertion: Each must-hold assumption has a measurable, falsifiable gate.
suggestions.reason: Strong as written but consider also asserting that at least one must-hold has a stated *pivot* path if the gate fails — this catches the failure mode where gates are precise but disconnected from build decisions. The current output does this well (M2's pivot, M1's pivot-on-hallucination), so it's a free signal worth grading.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-targeted for this skill — the IA assertion in particular discriminates clearly and the agent surfaced it both as a gated must-hold and as a justification in decision.md. Two small gaps: the SDK assertion can be satisfied by import-only code, and there's no explicit check that the spike was actually executed against real data (which is where the load-bearing F1 parser-trap finding came from).
